sentences.text: Inflammation has a significant role in atherosclerotic lesion development, which is driven by the release of local cytokines acting upon the endothelium and smooth muscle cells.
Janiques and colleagues investigated the anti-inflammatory and antioxidative effects of grapes in hemodialysis patients.
This was a double-blind placebo-controlled study in which 32 subjects undergoing hemodialysis were given either 500 milligrams of polyphenol from grape powder per day or a placebo for 5 weeks.

Subjects consuming the grape powder had an increase in glutathione peroxidase activity and no increase in C-reactive protein, which was observed in the placebo group, demonstrating the antioxidant potential of grapes in these patients.

Using the same study design with statin-treated patients and grape products, resveratrol-enriched grape extract and grape extract, in a high-risk population of 75 individuals with stable coronary artery disease and receiving a higher dose of statins, Tomé-Carneiro and colleagues reported an increase in the anti-inflammatory serum adiponectin and a decrease in the thrombogenic P A I-1 in the resveratrol-enriched grape extract group, in contrast to the grape extract and placebo groups, after 12 months of intervention.
Additionally, resveratrol-enriched grape extract downregulated pro-inflammatory gene expression in peripheral blood mononuclear cells extracted from the participants.
However, not all the published literature supports the anti-inflammatory effects of grapes.

In contrast to these studies, a meta-analysis on the effects of grape polyphenols on selected inflammatory markers by Haghighatdoost and colleagues reported no significant changes in C-reactive protein, I L-6, or T N F-alpha between the intervention and the control groups.
These results point to the need to conduct more clinical trials with a higher number of participants to increase our understanding of the effects of grape polyphenols on inflammation.
